content:
# Joshua Chapter 3 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 227-228 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_003_page_001.jpg`

---

### Preparation at the Jordan (vv. 1-6)

**1** Joshua rose early the next morning. They broke camp from Shittim and came to the Jordan — he and all the Sons of Israel — and camped there before crossing over.

**2** After three days, the officers went through the camp

**3** and gave the people these orders: "When you see the ark of the covenant of YHWH your God, carried by the Levitical priests, break camp and follow it.

**4** But keep a distance between yourselves and the ark — about two thousand cubits. Do not come any closer. That way, you will be able to see the route you need to take, since you have never traveled this way before."

**5** Joshua told the people, "Purify yourselves, because tomorrow YHWH is going to do something extraordinary among you."

**6** Then Joshua told the priests, "Lift up the ark of the covenant and go ahead of the people." So they lifted the ark of the covenant and took the lead.

---

### YHWH Speaks to Joshua — The Living God Marches Ahead (vv. 7-13)

**7** YHWH said to Joshua, "Today I will begin to elevate you in the sight of all Israel, so that they will know that I am with you just as I was with Moses.

**8** Give this command to the priests carrying the ark of the covenant: 'When you reach the edge of the Jordan's waters, walk in and stand there.'"

**9** Joshua said to the Sons of Israel, "Come here and listen to the words of YHWH your God."

**10** Then Joshua said, "This is how you will know that the living God is among you — and that he will without fail drive out from before you the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Hivites, the Perizzites, the Girgashites, the Amorites, and the Jebusites:

**11** The ark of the covenant of the Lord of all the earth is about to cross ahead of you into the Jordan.

**12** Now choose twelve men from the tribes of Israel — one from each tribe.

**13** The moment the feet of the priests carrying the ark of YHWH, the Lord of all the earth, come to rest in the Jordan's waters, the waters of the Jordan will be cut off. The water flowing downstream will stop and pile up in a single mass."

---

### The Crossing — The Waters Stand (vv. 14-17)

**14** So when the people broke camp to cross the Jordan, with the priests carrying the ark of the covenant out ahead of them,

**15** and when those carrying the ark reached the Jordan and the priests' feet touched the water's edge — the Jordan was in flood, overflowing its banks the whole harvest season —

**16** the water flowing downstream stopped. It rose up in a single mass far upstream, near the town of Adam, beside Zarethan. The water flowing down toward the sea of the Arabah — the Salt Sea — was completely cut off. And the people crossed over opposite Jericho.

**17** The priests carrying the ark of the covenant of YHWH stood firmly on dry ground in the middle of the Jordan while all Israel crossed over on dry ground, until the entire nation had finished crossing the Jordan.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — THE FINAL CAMP BEFORE THE PROMISED LAND (Heb. *vayyashkem Yehoshua babboqer vayyis'u mehashShittim vayyavo'u ad-hayYarden hu vekhol-benei Yisra'el vayyalinu sham terem ya'avoru*, וַיַּשְׁכֵּם יְהוֹשֻׁעַ בַּבֹּקֶר וַיִּסְעוּ מֵהַשִּׁטִּים וַיָּבֹאוּ עַד־הַיַּרְדֵּן הוּא וְכׇל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיָּלִנוּ שָׁם טֶרֶם יַעֲבֹרוּ):** The verb *vayyashkem* (וישכם, from *shakham*, שכם, "to rise early, to do something at dawn") is a verb of urgency and purpose. Abraham *shakham* before the binding of Isaac (Gen. 22:3). Jacob *shakham* before his encounter with Esau (Gen. 32:1). Moses *shakham* before confronting Pharaoh (Exo. 8:16). Whenever a biblical leader rises early, what follows is consequential. Joshua rises early because what is about to happen has been forty years in the making.

Shittim (הַשִּׁטִּים, "the acacias") is the last encampment east of the Jordan (Num. 25:1; 33:49) — the place of Balaam's curse-turned-blessing, the place of the Baal-peor disaster, the final staging ground. The name itself is fitting: the acacia is the tree of the wilderness, the wood from which the ark of the covenant was built (Exo. 25:10). Israel departs from the place named after the tree that frames God's presence, following the box made from that same wood into the river.

The verb *vayyalinu* (וילנו, from *lin*, לין, "to lodge, to spend the night") marks a pause. They do not cross immediately. They arrive at the Jordan and wait. The pause is not hesitation — it is the narrative holding its breath. Everything from Exodus through Deuteronomy has been moving toward this riverbank. The crossing cannot be casual. It requires preparation, both ritual and emotional.

**v.3 — THE ARK LEADS (Heb. *kir'otkhem et aron berit-YHWH Eloheikhem vehaKohanim haLeviyyim nos'im oto ve'attem tis'u mimmeqomkhem vahalakhtem acharav*, כִּרְאוֹתְכֶם אֵת אֲרוֹן בְּרִית־יְהוָה אֱלֹהֵיכֶם וְהַכֹּהֲנִים הַלְוִיִּם נֹשְׂאִים אֹתוֹ וְאַתֶּם תִּסְעוּ מִמְּקוֹמְכֶם וַהֲלַכְתֶּם אַחֲרָיו):** The phrase *haKohanim haLeviyyim* (הכהנים הלוים, "the priests, the Levites") is the characteristic Deuteronomistic phrase that identifies the priestly class with the tribe of Levi. The ark is not a magic box — it is the visible sign of YHWH's covenant presence. The instruction is simple: when you see the ark move, you move. The people do not lead; the presence leads. The verb *vahalakhtem acharav* (והלכתם אחריו, "and you shall walk after it") uses the same construction as "walking after YHWH" — following the ark is following God.

**v.4 — TWO THOUSAND CUBITS: THE DISTANCE OF AWE (Heb. *akh rachoq yihyeh beineikhem uveinav ke'alpayim ammah bammiddah al-tiqrevu elav*, אַךְ רָחוֹק יִהְיֶה בֵּינֵיכֶם וּבֵינָיו כְּאַלְפַּיִם אַמָּה בַּמִּדָּה אַל־תִּקְרְבוּ אֵלָיו):** The commanded distance — approximately one thousand meters — serves two purposes. First, practical: it keeps the ark visible as a guide to a massive column of people. Second, theological: it preserves sacral distance. The ark is the footstool of YHWH's throne (1 Chr. 28:2). You do not crowd the throne. The phrase *al-tiqrevu elav* (אל תקרבו אליו, "do not come near it") echoes the prohibition at Sinai (Exo. 19:12). The same God who descended in fire on the mountain now descends in the river. The same distance is required.

The reason given — *ki lo avartem badderekh mittmol shilshom* (כי לא עברתם בדרך מתמול שלשום, "for you have not passed this way before") — uses the Hebrew idiom *mittmol shilshom* ("from yesterday and the day before" = "previously"). They need to see where the ark goes because this is entirely uncharted territory. No Israelite alive has walked this road. The last generation that approached the land turned back (Num. 14). This generation must follow a guide through terrain — physical and spiritual — that is utterly new to them.

**v.5 — "PURIFY YOURSELVES" (Heb. *hitqaddashu ki makhar ya'aseh YHWH beqirbekhem nifla'ot*, הִתְקַדָּשׁוּ כִּי מָחָר יַעֲשֶׂה יְהוָה בְּקִרְבְּכֶם נִפְלָאוֹת):** The Hitpael *hitqaddashu* (התקדשו, "sanctify/purify yourselves") — reflexive of *qadash* (קדש, "to be holy, to be set apart") — implies ritual preparation: washing garments, abstaining from sexual contact, avoiding sources of impurity (cf. Exo. 19:10-15). The parallel with Sinai is unmistakable. Before the theophany at Sinai, Moses told the people to consecrate themselves (Exo. 19:10). Now Joshua gives the identical command before the theophany at the Jordan. The crossing is not merely a military operation; it is a sacred event.

The word *nifla'ot* (נפלאות, "wonders") — the Niphal participle of *pala'* (פלא, "to be extraordinary, to be beyond comprehension") — is the standard term for God's saving acts: the plagues (Exo. 3:20), the Sinai revelation (Exo. 34:10), the wilderness provision. The word does not mean "miracle" in the modern sense of a violation of natural law. It means something that exceeds human capacity to achieve or fully understand. What is about to happen at the Jordan belongs to the same category as the parting of the sea.

**v.7 — "AS I WAS WITH MOSES, I WILL BE WITH YOU" (Heb. *hayyom hazzeh achel gaddelkha be'einei khol-Yisra'el asher yed'un ki ka'asher hayiti im-Mosheh ehyeh immakh*, הַיּוֹם הַזֶּה אָחֵל גַּדֶּלְךָ בְּעֵינֵי כׇל־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר יֵדְעוּן כִּי כַּאֲשֶׁר הָיִיתִי עִם־מֹשֶׁה אֶהְיֶה עִמָּךְ):** The verb *achel* (אחל, Hiphil of *chalal*, חלל, "to begin") marks a turning point: this is the day Joshua's authority becomes visible. The verb *gaddelkha* (גדלך, Piel of *gadal*, גדל, "to make great") is not about personal fame — it is about public authentication. Israel needs to know that the same divine power that backed Moses now backs Joshua. Without this authentication, Joshua is merely a general; with it, he is Moses' successor.

The divine formula *ehyeh immakh* (אהיה עמך, "I will be with you") uses the same verb *ehyeh* (אהיה, "I will be") that YHWH used to reveal his name at the burning bush (Exo. 3:14). The "I AM" who spoke to Moses at the bush now speaks to Joshua at the river. The name is not a philosophical proposition about divine existence; it is a promise of divine presence. "I will be" means "I will be there."

The form *yed'un* (ידעון) with the paragogic nun is archaic or elevated — lending weight and formality to the declaration. This is not casual speech. YHWH speaks in his most solemn register.

**v.10 — THE LIVING GOD AND SEVEN NATIONS (Heb. *bezot ted'un ki El chai beqirbekhem vehoresh yorish mippneikhem et-haKena'ani ve'et-haChitti ve'et-haChivvi ve'et-haPerizzi ve'et-haGirgashi veha'Emori vehaYevusi*, בְּזֹאת תֵּדְעוּן כִּי אֵל חַי בְּקִרְבְּכֶם וְהוֹרֵשׁ יוֹרִישׁ מִפְּנֵיכֶם אֶת־הַכְּנַעֲנִי וְאֶת־הַחִתִּי וְאֶת־הַחִוִּי וְאֶת־הַפְּרִזִּי וְאֶת־הַגִּרְגָּשִׁי וְהָאֱמֹרִי וְהַיְבוּסִי):** The title *El chai* (אל חי, "the living God") is theologically charged. The nations they are about to face worship gods represented by carved stone and cast metal — gods that do not breathe, speak, or act. The God of Israel is *chai* — alive, breathing, active, present. The contrast is not abstract theology but military reality: a living God acts; dead idols do not.

The construction *vehoresh yorish* (והורש יורש) — infinitive absolute (*horesh*) + finite verb (*yorish*) — is the Hebrew construction of absolute certainty. Rendered woodenly: "driving-out, he will drive out." There is no "maybe" in this grammar. The driving-out is guaranteed. PNEUMA renders this as "without fail" to capture the force without the awkwardness of a doubled verb in English.

The seven-nation list (Canaanites, Hittites, Hivites, Perizzites, Girgashites, Amorites, Jebusites) is the standard catalogue of the land's inhabitants (Deut. 7:1). Seven is the number of completeness. The listing of all seven signals total dispossession — not one people group will remain to contest Israel's occupation of the land.

**v.11 — "THE LORD OF ALL THE EARTH" (Heb. *hinneh aron habberit Adon kol-ha'arets over lifneikhem baYYarden*, הִנֵּה אֲרוֹן הַבְּרִית אֲדוֹן כׇּל־הָאָרֶץ עֹבֵר לִפְנֵיכֶם בַּיַּרְדֵּן):** The title *Adon kol-ha'arets* (אדון כל הארץ, "Lord of all the earth") is rare and powerful — appearing also in Micah 4:13, Zechariah 4:14, 6:5, and Psalm 97:5. Note that this is *Adon* (אדון, "lord, sovereign, master"), not the Tetragrammaton YHWH. The title makes a geopolitical claim: the God whose ark is about to enter the Jordan is not a local deity confined to the wilderness or to Sinai. He is the sovereign of the entire earth — including the land of Canaan, currently occupied by the seven nations just listed. The God who rules all the earth is about to claim one particular piece of it for his people.

The particle *hinneh* (הנה, "behold!") demands attention — it is the narrator's spotlight, the verbal equivalent of pointing. Look: the ark is moving. The living God is entering the river.

**v.13 — "THE WATERS WILL PILE UP IN A SINGLE MASS" (Heb. *mei haYYarden yikkaretun hammayim hayyoredim milema'elah veya'amdu ned echad*, מֵי הַיַּרְדֵּן יִכָּרֵתוּן הַמַּיִם הַיֹּרְדִים מִלְמָעְלָה וְיַעַמְדוּ נֵד אֶחָד):** The verb *yikkaretun* (יכרתון, Niphal of *karat*, כרת, "to cut off, to cut a covenant") with the paragogic nun is both solemn and deliberately evocative. The root *karat* is the same verb used for "cutting" a covenant (*karat berit*). The waters of the Jordan are "cut" just as covenants are "cut" — and the ark crossing the river is itself the *aron habberit*, the "ark of the covenant." The covenant-making God cuts the waters as he cuts his commitments: decisively, irrevocably.

The noun *ned* (נד, "heap, mound") appears in only two other places: Exodus 15:8 ("the floods stood up as a *ned*") and Psalm 78:13 ("he made the waters stand as a *ned*"). The literary echo is deliberate. What happened at the sea is about to happen at the river. The generation that heard their parents tell the story of the Reed Sea is about to live their own version of it. The God who piled up the sea now piles up the Jordan. History is rhyming.

**v.15 — THE JORDAN AT FLOOD STAGE (Heb. *vehaYYarden male al-kol-gedotav kol yemei qatsir*, וְהַיַּרְדֵּן מָלֵא עַל־כׇּל־גְּדוֹתָיו כֹּל יְמֵי קָצִיר):** This parenthetical note is not filler — it is the narrator's way of heightening the miracle. The word *male* (מלא, "full") and *gedotav* (גדותיו, "its banks") tell us the Jordan was not a trickle that could be waded. During the barley harvest (March-April), snowmelt from Mount Hermon swells the Jordan to its maximum. The river is at its most powerful, most dangerous, most impassable — and this is the moment YHWH chooses to cross. The miracle is calibrated to the obstacle. The greater the flood, the greater the display of power.

The verb *nitbelu* (נטבלו, Niphal of *taval*, טבל, "to dip, to immerse") describing the priests' feet touching the water is the same root from which later Jewish *tevilah* (ritual immersion) and ultimately Christian "baptism" (via Greek *baptizo*) derive their vocabulary. The priests' feet are "immersed" in the Jordan — the smallest contact with the water triggers the greatest response from God.

**v.16 — THE WATERS STOP AT ADAM (Heb. *vayyamdu hammayim hayyoredim milema'lah qamu ned-echad harcheq me'od be'Adam ha'ir asher mitstsad Tsaretan*, וַיַּעַמְדוּ הַמַּיִם הַיֹּרְדִים מִלְמַעְלָה קָמוּ נֵד־אֶחָד הַרְחֵק מְאֹד בְּאָדָם הָעִיר אֲשֶׁר מִצַּד צָרְתָן):** The double verb *vayyamdu... qamu* (ויעמדו...קמו, "stood still... rose up") is emphatic: the water did not merely slow — it stopped and then piled vertically. The phrase *harcheq me'od* (הרחק מאד, "very far away") tells us the damming occurred far upstream, near the town of *Adam* (אדם, identified with Tell ed-Damiyeh, approximately thirty kilometers north of the crossing point). The entire stretch of riverbed between Adam and the crossing point opposite Jericho drained dry.

The Ketiv (written text) reads *me'Adam* (מאדם, "from Adam") while the Qere (reading tradition) reads *be'Adam* (באדם, "at Adam"). The LXX (Vaticanus) reads a different place name entirely — Kariathiarim — suggesting textual variation in the geographical tradition.

The downstream waters — those flowing toward *yam ha'Aravah, yam-haMelach* (ים הערבה ים המלח, "the sea of the Arabah, the Salt Sea," i.e., the Dead Sea) — are described with two verbs: *tammu nikhrethu* (תמו נכרתו, "were finished, were cut off"). The doubling is emphatic: the water did not just stop arriving — it was entirely exhausted, totally severed. The riverbed was bare.

**v.17 — DRY GROUND IN THE MIDDLE OF THE JORDAN (Heb. *vayyamdu haKohanim nos'ei ha'Aron berit-YHWH becharavah betokh haYYarden hakhen vekhol-Yisra'el overim becharavah ad asher-tammu khol-haggoi la'avor et-haYYarden*, וַיַּעַמְדוּ הַכֹּהֲנִים נֹשְׂאֵי הָאָרוֹן בְּרִית־יְהוָה בֶּחָרָבָה בְּתוֹךְ הַיַּרְדֵּן הָכֵן וְכׇל־יִשְׂרָאֵל עֹבְרִים בֶּחָרָבָה עַד אֲשֶׁר־תַּמּוּ כׇּל־הַגּוֹי לַעֲבֹר אֶת־הַיַּרְדֵּן):** The word *charavah* (חרבה, "dry ground") — from the root *charev* (חרב, "to be dry, to be desolate") — is the same word used for the dry ground that appeared at the Reed Sea (Exo. 14:21). The echo is unmistakable: what happened at the sea under Moses happens now at the river under Joshua. The succession is authenticated by the repetition. If the parting of the sea proved Moses, the parting of the river proves Joshua.

The adverb *hakhen* (הכן, "firmly, securely, prepared") tells us the priests did not stand nervously shifting in mud. They stood immovable — anchored, stable, unshaken — while an entire nation filed past them through the riverbed. The ark and its bearers are the fixed point around which the crossing orbits. They enter first and leave last (4:18). They are the hinge of the miracle.

The shift from *Yisra'el* (ישראל, "Israel") to *haggoi* (הגוי, "the nation") in the final clause is significant. Throughout the Torah, Israel is identified by tribal names, clan structures, family lines — *benei Yisra'el*, the "Sons of Israel." Here, at the moment of crossing, the text calls them *haggoi* — "the nation." The tribal confederation becomes a single political entity as it crosses the river. What enters the Jordan is a collection of families descended from a man named Israel. What emerges from the Jordan is a nation.

---

**The theology of Joshua 3 — the Jordan divided, the ark leading, a new exodus:**

This chapter is the literary and theological twin of Exodus 14. The parallels are systematic: waters part, the people cross on dry ground (*charavah*), the waters stand in a heap (*ned*), and a leader is authenticated before the people. The narrator wants the reader to understand that the God who brought Israel out of Egypt is the same God who brings Israel into Canaan. The exodus is not complete until the entry is accomplished. The sea and the river are bookends of the same story.

But there is a crucial difference. At the sea, Moses raised his staff and an east wind blew all night (Exo. 14:21). Here, no staff is raised and no wind blows. The priests simply walk into the water carrying the ark, and the river stops. The mechanism is more intimate — not a cosmic wind but human feet touching water. The contact between the sacred object and the natural obstacle is enough. The ark does what the staff did, because the presence does what the power did.

The two-thousand-cubit gap (v.4) introduces a tension that runs through the entire chapter: the people must follow the presence, but they cannot approach it too closely. God is near enough to guide, distant enough to remain holy. This is the paradox at the heart of Israelite theology — the God who dwells among his people (*beqirbekhem*, v.5, v.10) is also the God who must not be approached (*al-tiqrevu*, v.4). The crossing of the Jordan is an exercise in guided distance: close enough to see, far enough to revere.

The title *Adon kol-ha'arets* ("Lord of all the earth"), used twice (vv.11, 13), is the theological key to the chapter. Israel is about to enter a land filled with other peoples who worship other gods. The claim embedded in this title is that those gods are irrelevant. YHWH is not competing for jurisdiction; he already holds it. The Lord of all the earth enters his own territory. The Canaanites are not defending their land from an invader — they are occupying land that belongs to the God who made it. The seven-nation list (v.10) is not a threat assessment; it is a dispossession notice.

Finally, the chapter's most profound image: the priests standing in the middle of a dry riverbed while a nation walks past them. They are the still point. They hold the ark — the seat of God's presence — and they do not move until every last person has crossed. The living God, represented by his ark, enters the danger first and leaves it last. He stands in the place of risk so that his people can walk through safety. The river is held back not by engineering but by presence. As long as the ark stands in the Jordan, the waters cannot return. The crossing depends entirely on the God who holds the flood at bay.

— *PNEUMA*
